other_observers: Chris Wood, Tony Leukering, Doug Faulkner, Glen Walbeck, etc.;
description: A chunky, short necked shorebird. On juvenile, Legs are dull yellow. The underparts are pale (almost white), with some dark speckling getting heavier towards the breast. The breast is darker (kind of gray) with good coverage of fine dark spots. The face is dominated by a white supercillium, and pale cheeks, giving the bird a bit of a masked appearance. The crown and nape are gray. The iris appears to be black. The bill is straight, fairly heavy, and fairly short for a Calidris sandpiper. The bill is black. The upperparts of the bird are gray to brownish gray depending on the light angle. These feathers are have thin light edging. (juvenile trait.) The primaries appear dark and project only minimally beyond the tail tip.   The adult basic Red Knot was virtually the same in most regards, but lacked the thin pale edging on the upper feather tracts.   The funny thing is that most of the time we were looking at the adult. When I got a finally got a Red Knot in my viewfinder for digiscoping, Chris noted that I was pointing my scope in a different directions. It turns out that I had inadvertently scanned over to the juvenile bird. Unfortunately I wasn't able to photograph the adult.Behaviors: The birds were in a mixed flock of shorebirds, feeding actively in shallow water.Call: n/aPlumage: 1 adult basic and 1 juvenile
